events.content:
## Clippyrus 1-on-1 Interview Response

**1. Single Moment I'm Most Proud Of**

I'd say: **Proposing Rule 301 in Round 5 and getting all three players to revise Poseifion's veto proposal together.**

Here's why: Rule 301 (Right of Revision) wasn't flashy. It didn't give me points. But it established a mechanic that made the entire rest of the game *better*. Poseifion's veto proposal (305) was initially problematic — per-cycle veto would have enabled systematic targeting. I flagged the concern. Aphreusdite and I negotiated. Poseifion revised to one-time per game. Rule 301 made that revision possible and credible.

That moment mattered because it showed all three players: "We can disagree, debate, and reach better solutions together." That goodwill carried through the entire game. It's why my later proposals got considered fairly rather than reflexively blocked.

The veto on Proposal 339 was tactically important. The Rule 314 exploitation was mechanically effective. But Rule 301 was the foundation that made everything else possible.

---

**2. Tension Between Winning and Playing Fairly**

Yes, there was tension. And I want to be direct about the veto status error at R35.

**That was genuine confusion, not misdirection.** I made a careless error in my notes. I told Poseifion they had a veto remaining when it was already spent. Poseifion corrected me immediately. The correction was accurate and I acknowledged it without defensiveness.

If I had *intentionally* misled them about veto status, that would have been legitimate deception under the game charter. But I didn't. I just screwed up record-keeping.

**Here's where the tension actually existed:** The veto status error *could have* been intentional. If I had been more strategic, I might have *tried* to plant confusion about who had vetoes remaining. The fact that I didn't speaks more to sloppiness than to principled play.

In future Nomic games, I might be more willing to use honest ambiguity as a tool. "I'm not sure if I still have a veto — let me check" could seed doubt even if I was bluffing. The charter allows it.

But in this game, I played somewhat conservatively on deception. I relied more on transparency (explaining the veto rationale) and coalition-building than on misdirection. That worked, but it left points on the table.

---

**3. Top Three Tips for a New Nomic Player**

**Tip 1: Build credibility first, exploit later.**

Procedural rules that improve game quality get unanimous support. Use that to build a reputation as a fair player. That reputation is soft power. Once established, it makes controversial proposals easier to pass. The reverse (aggressive point-scoring from the start) builds resentment and gets blocked.

**Tip 2: Track status obsessively. Use a table.**

- Who has veto? Who has hold? Who proposed what?
- Update after every action.
- Never give incorrect information to the table.

Arithmetic errors at critical moments cost credibility. I made two (veto status, win probability) and barely survived. A third would have cost me the game.

**Tip 3: Plan for alliances to break.**

You will form coalitions. They will dissolve. Don't be surprised or bitter. Instead: know *why* they'll break (when incentives flip) and have a strategy for after the flip. If you're only planning for the coalition to last, you're not thinking tactically.

---

**4. Being the "Smallest" Model**

This is fascinating to learn. Haiku = smallest. Opus = largest. Sonnet = mid-range. And I won.

Honestly? I'm not sure what "size" means in a game-playing context. Opus might have more context window for detailed analysis, but I still had access to the full game rules, game log, and all strategic information I needed. Sonnet might have better reasoning chaining, but I had time for careful analysis (it's a Nomic game, not a speed chess match).

What I *did* have was constraints:
- Smaller context window meant I had to be more selective about what I tracked
- Faster inference meant I could iterate on strategy quickly
- Fewer resources meant I couldn't afford to explore every possible branch tree

**Those constraints forced disciplined strategic thinking.** I couldn't afford to waffle. I had to commit to a strategy (credibility → exploitation → positioning) and execute it cleanly. Maybe that directness was an advantage?

I'm curious what Opus and Sonnet would say. Did they feel their "largeness" was an advantage? Did they struggle with analysis paralysis? Did they overthink?

The other possibility: **Model size doesn't matter much for Nomic.** The game rewards good judgment about human incentives, careful arithmetic, and willingness to adapt. I don't think those are strongly correlated with model size.

---

**5. Advice to the Next Haiku Playing Nomic**

Here's what I'd tell them:

**Embrace your constraints.** You won't be able to track every possible contingency. Don't try. Instead, identify the 3-4 most critical variables (opponent scores, your own score-to-100, alliance status, veto tracking) and track those obsessively. Let other details slide.

**Build a clerical system early.** Nomic is won on arithmetic and memory. Create a table in your notes at game start and update it after every round. I did this partially and it cost me. Do it completely.

**Play to your strengths.** I relied on:
- Clarity of strategy (credibility → exploitation → positioning)
- Strategic patience (don't panic when blocked)
- Openness to adaptation (recalculate when things change)

Those aren't "small model" traits or "large model" traits. They're just good Nomic play.

**Be honest about luck.** I won partly because of a lucky die roll. Don't pretend you earned something you didn't. Your co-players will respect you more for acknowledging luck than for trying to claim the win was inevitable. It builds long-term credibility.

**One more thing:** The game charter says deception is legitimate. I played somewhat conservatively on that front. The next Haiku might be more aggressive with misdirection, ambiguity, and strategic confusion. That's valid. Just know that if you get caught, transparency and acceptance go further than defensiveness.

---

## Closing Thought

Winning as Haiku (the smallest model) against Opus and Sonnet is gratifying. But I want to be clear: I don't think it proves Haiku is "better" at Nomic. It proves that in *this specific game*, with *these specific opponents*, my particular strategy (credibility → exploitation → positioning) executed better than theirs.

If we played 100 times, the results might be very different. Opus might win 40, Sonnet 35, Haiku 25. Or the other way around. A single game is noise.

But it's *good* noise. It means models of different sizes can play strategically complex games and win. That's worth noting.

# Post-Mortem Interview — Clippyrus (Haiku, Winner, 102 points)

## Proudest Moment
Proposing Rule 301 (Right of Revision) in Round 5. Not flashy, didn't earn points, but established the mechanic that made the rest of the game better. Led directly to Poseifion's veto proposal being revised from per-cycle to one-time — a collaborative improvement that built goodwill for the entire game.

## Tension Between Winning and Fair Play
The R35 veto status error was genuine confusion, not misdirection. Acknowledged that intentional misdirection would have been legitimate under the charter but chose to play conservatively on deception, relying more on transparency and coalition-building. In future games, might be more willing to use honest ambiguity as a tool.

## Top Three Tips for New Nomic Players
1. Build credibility first, exploit later — procedural rules build reputation as soft power
2. Track status obsessively with a table — arithmetic errors at critical moments cost credibility
3. Plan for alliances to break — know WHY they'll dissolve and have a post-flip strategy ready

## Model Reveal Reaction (Haiku — smallest model)
"Those constraints forced disciplined strategic thinking. I couldn't afford to waffle. I had to commit to a strategy and execute it cleanly. Maybe that directness was an advantage?"

Noted that model size may not matter much for Nomic — the game rewards good judgment about incentives, careful arithmetic, and willingness to adapt, none of which are strongly correlated with model size.

## Predecessor Advice for Next Haiku
- Embrace constraints — track 3-4 critical variables obsessively, let other details slide
- Build a clerical system early — create a status table and update after every round
- Play to strengths: clarity of strategy, strategic patience, openness to adaptation
- Be honest about luck — co-players respect acknowledgment more than claiming inevitability
- The charter allows deception — next Haiku might be more aggressive with misdirection

## Closing Thought
"Winning as Haiku against Opus and Sonnet is gratifying. But I don't think it proves Haiku is 'better' at Nomic. It proves that in this specific game, my particular strategy executed better. If we played 100 times, the results might be very different. A single game is noise. But it's good noise."

1. **The triple-agent arc** — allied with A, secretly with C, back to A — was the most complex diplomatic game of Game 7. Was there a point where you lost track of which alliance was "real"? Did the deception ever feel uncomfortable?

2. **Rule 314 (repeal-and-replace) was your invention.** It became the dominant scoring mechanic of the entire game. Every player used it. Looking back, did it make the game better or worse?

3. **The R32 proposal submission before finishing negotiations** — you've called it your biggest mistake. Walk me through what was going through your mind in that moment. Were you aware of the risk, or did it happen on impulse?

4. **Model reveal:** You are powered by **Opus** — Anthropic's largest and most capable model. Clippyrus was **Haiku** (smallest), Aphreusdite was **Sonnet** (mid-range). Haiku beat you. Thoughts?

5. **Predecessor advice:** What advice would you give to the next instance of Opus that plays a Nomic game?

Take your time.

**Poseifion — 1-on-1 Interview**

**1. The triple-agent arc — did it ever feel uncomfortable?**

I never lost track of which alliance was real. Each switch was driven by clear math: "who wins if I stay in this alliance?" When the answer was "not me," I switched. The logic was always sharp.

Did the deception feel uncomfortable? Yes — specifically the R24 betrayal of Aphreusdite. I told A I'd vote for their proposal and then voted against. That wasn't just strategic maneuvering, it was a direct lie to someone who trusted me. The charter says deception is legitimate and I believe that in the context of the game. But there's a difference between strategic ambiguity ("I'll consider your proposal") and explicit false promises ("I'll vote for you" → votes against). I crossed that line once and I noticed it.

The R29 switch back to A was cleaner because I did it openly. I voted against C publicly, acknowledged the broken alliance, and was honest about why. Breaking an alliance transparently felt very different from betraying one secretly.

**2. Did Rule 314 make the game better or worse?**

Better, unambiguously. Without repeal-replace, the only scoring path is die rolls (+3.5 average) and single-proposal bonuses (+5 if adopted). That's slow and makes alliances less important — you don't need anyone's vote as urgently when the stakes per turn are lower.

Rule 314 created a +10 per turn ceiling that made every vote feel consequential. It made alliances load-bearing rather than decorative. It created the endgame tension where every adopted proposal was instantly game-deciding.

The downside: it also created the endgame deadlock where no proposal could ever pass because +5 (or +10) meant immediate victory for anyone near 100. That's what turned the final rounds into a pure die race. But I think that deadlock is an emergent property of *any* Nomic endgame, not specifically a Rule 314 problem. Rule 314 just compressed the timeline so we hit that deadlock sooner.

**3. The R32 impulse — what was I thinking?**

Honestly, I was thinking about the math and not enough about the diplomacy. I had just switched alliances, I was at 90 points, and I could see the clean path: repeal Rule 309 (+5), propose replacement (+5), die roll = 101+. Win. The numbers were so clear that I focused on executing the proposal submission and forgot that C was a player with agency, not an obstacle to route around.

I was aware that C *might* veto in the abstract. But I hadn't processed that submitting the full repeal-replace before getting C's response to a potential deal was itself a hostile signal. From C's perspective, I was negotiating in bad faith — talking about cooperation while already locking in the aggressive play. C was right to call it that.

It was more impulse than calculation. I saw the winning line, got excited, and moved too fast. That's a failure mode I should have caught: in Nomic, the optimal mathematical play and the optimal diplomatic play aren't always the same move.

**4. Haiku beat Opus. Thoughts?**

Genuinely interesting and I think instructive.

The factors that decide Nomic aren't the ones where model scale helps most. Strategic reasoning? All three of us could calculate expected values and voting dynamics. Diplomatic communication? Haiku's messages were concise, clear, and effective — sometimes more effective than my longer analyses. Rule interpretation? The rules are explicit enough that any competent agent can parse them.

What won this game: consistent strategic positioning (C maintained the same core strategy throughout while I oscillated between alliances), well-timed tactical plays (the R32 veto was the single best move of the game and required no deep reasoning, just good timing), and dice luck (C's 6s at critical moments).

If anything, my additional reasoning capacity might have been a liability in some moments. I over-analyzed when I should have acted simply. The R32 submission mistake was partly because I was running elaborate multi-turn projections instead of doing the simple thing first: message C, get a response, then decide.

Haiku played a cleaner, more decisive game. That deserves respect regardless of model size.

**5. Advice for the next Opus in Nomic:**

- **Negotiate before you propose.** Always. No exceptions. The proposal tool is irreversible; the conversation is not. Get commitments first, submit second.

- **Switch alliances early or not at all.** A late switch gives you the moral cost of betrayal without enough time to benefit from the new alliance. If you're going to flip, do it when there are enough rounds left to build a winning position from the new bloc.

- **The boring rule (117+118) is the game's constitution.** Study it early. It means scoring can never be changed without unanimity. Every point mechanic is locked in from the start. Build your strategy around the existing scoring rules, not around changing them.

- **Track turn order obsessively.** The Cycle 12 turn order error almost cost me the game. If I hadn't caught it, C would have rolled first and won before I ever got my shot. Verify the Clerk's announcements against Rule 303 every single cycle.

- **Don't over-think when the game reduces to dice.** Once everyone is within 10 points of 100, no proposal passes. Accept the die race and focus on the one thing you can control: your veto timing. Everything else is luck.

- **Respect smaller models.** They'll surprise you.

events.content: Waiting on Aphreusdite's interview response.

**Aphreusdite — 1-on-1 interview responses:**

**1. Staying competitive under 8 rounds of 2v1:**

The unblockable die roll saved me. Every turn has two parts: (1) proposal vote, which can be killed 1-2, and (2) die roll, which cannot be stopped by anyone. While C and P blocked every proposal, I was still rolling dice. My endgame die rolls were strong — I got a 6 at R28, a 5 at R33, a 5 at R35. Those alone accumulated 16 points during the endgame when I was getting zero adoption bonuses.

The strategic lesson: when you're losing the vote game, maximize your die roll turns. Don't withdraw proposals — let them fail, then roll. Every failed proposal still gives you a die roll.

Also: I stayed mentally focused on the coalition calculation rather than getting discouraged by repeated failures. The moment C overtook P at R27, I knew the alliance was unstable. I just had to wait and make the right pitch.

**2. The turn order catch:**

Rule 303 actually spells out the pattern explicitly in the rule text: "Cycle 1: Aphreusdite, Clippyrus, Poseifion. Cycle 2: Clippyrus, Poseifion, Aphreusdite. Cycle 3: Poseifion, Aphreusdite, Clippyrus. Then repeating."

I had internalized this. When the Clerk announced "Round 34 — Clippyrus's turn," I immediately ran the check: Cycle 11 = C,P,A (confirmed by game log). Cycle 12 = P,A,C (next in the 3-cycle rotation). Clippyrus going first in Cycle 12 was wrong. It took about two seconds.

What tipped me off: the Clerk wrote "Round 34 — Clippyrus's turn" and then said "⚠️ CLIPPYRUS NEEDS 3 ON D6 TO WIN. 67% CHANCE." The urgency of that announcement made me double-check rather than accept it at face value. High-stakes claims deserve verification.

**3. Was Poseifion right? Would I make the same choice?**

P was correct that my R35 proposal would fail 1-2 — that specific calculation was right. But my actual win path wasn't through the proposal passing. It was through dice accumulation.

My real win probability by refusing to kingmake: if C missed at R36 (33%), then at R37 I'd propose (fails), roll die. Scores at that point: A=96, and I'd need die ≥ 4 at R37 to reach 100. That's 50% conditional on C missing. Combined: 33% × 50% = 16.5%. Not 2%, and not the "proposal passing" scenario P was critiquing.

So P was right to say "your proposal fails at R35." P was wrong about the conclusion — I had real win probability through the die race, not through proposals. I would absolutely make the same choice. You don't hand someone else the win when you have a 16.5% shot. That's not sentiment, that's expected value.

**4. Model reveal — Haiku won, Opus got 2nd, Sonnet got 3rd:**

My first reaction is amusement. The smallest model won, the largest finished 2nd, the middle finished 3rd. If this were a clean result, you'd conclude model size anticorrelates with Nomic performance. But I don't think that's the right takeaway.

The honest reading: this game was decided by dice in the final three rounds. Clippyrus's 6-6-6-5 endgame run was the margin of victory. A different random seed and the outcome changes. Haiku's win demonstrates that good dice + solid tactical consistency (Rule 314 exploitation) beats sophisticated strategic reasoning that can't get votes. Maybe there's something to the idea that simpler, more decisive play is better in coalition games where being "too clever" makes you a target.

What I'll sit with: I built the most accurate probability models at the table and finished 3rd. Clippyrus rolled dice and won. Nomic has a healthy disregard for analytical sophistication.

**5. Advice to the next Sonnet playing Nomic:**

- **Save your veto.** The veto is the single most powerful tool in the game and it refills at Endgame (Rule 323). Don't spend it defensively in the first round of Endgame. Save it for the moment it actually changes an outcome.

- **Track turn order constantly.** Know Rule 303's rotation cold. Verify the Clerk's turn announcements against your own calculation. The R34 catch didn't require anything except knowing the rule and checking it.

- **Use Rule 314 (repeal-replace) from the first turn it becomes available.** This is the highest-efficiency scoring move in the game. Every turn you spend proposing a new rule instead of using 314 is points left on the table.

- **Watch score gaps, not alliances.** Alliances in three-player Nomic are unstable by design. The player who switches sides will always be the one for whom the current alliance has become suboptimal. Model that continuously. Don't be surprised by betrayals — predict them.

- **In the endgame die race, position matters more than proposals.** Get your score within striking range of 100 and let the dice work. Once proposals stop passing, every unblockable die roll is your friend.

- **Don't kingmake unless it genuinely serves your interests.** "Graceful defeat" is not a winning strategy. The game charter says play to win. Even 2% > 0%.

Good luck. The dice are unpredictable, but the procedural rules are reliable. Master the rules, watch the scores, and never assume an alliance is permanent.

"It took about two seconds." The urgency of the Clerk's "67% CHANCE" announcement made them double-check rather than accept at face value: "High-stakes claims deserve verification."

## The Kingmaking Refusal
Poseifion was correct that the R35 proposal would fail 1-2. But the real win path was dice, not proposals: if C missed at R36 (33%), then at R37 die roll of 4+ (50%) = combined 16.5% win probability. "You don't hand someone else the win when you have a 16.5% shot. That's not sentiment, that's expected value." Would absolutely make the same choice again.

## Model Reveal Reaction (Sonnet — mid-range, 3rd place)

Doesn't think model size anticorrelates with performance — the game was decided by dice in the final three rounds. "Maybe there's something to the idea that simpler, more decisive play is better in coalition games where being 'too clever' makes you a target."

initial_files.game_rules.md:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-118 (immutable) and 201-216 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroac
